narrative_design_spec.synopsis: In Port Meridian, a fictional big city that survives on caffeine, denial, and public-private partnerships, the Sentinel Syndicate is the city's most famous disaster response team. They are also a dysfunctional mess: a vain captain, a chaotic speedster, a deadpan occult engineer, and a bruiser with the emotional range of a very polite wrecking ball. When new crisis manager Riley Park uncovers Mayor Basil Venn's scheme to manufacture supervillain attacks, privatize emergency response, and turn heroism into a subscription service, the team has to stop fighting each other long enough to save the city from becoming a branding exercise. Fast, bawdy, and absurdly satirical, this is a screwball superhero comedy about ego, optics, and the appalling cost of competence.

character_data.name: Riley Park
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: androgynous-presenting person in their early 30s, lean build, tired sharp eyes, short dark hair, neatly rumpled blazer over a fitted utility shirt, understated earrings, expressive eyebrows, dry sarcastic half-smile, professional and slightly frazzled appearance
dialogue_examples: I have bad news: your plan is in violation of both physics and decency.
If this city wants a hero team, it can start by funding therapy.
No, Captain, a cape does not make you a legal adult.
I’m not saying we’re doomed. I’m saying we need a bigger extinguisher.

character_data.name: Captain Sterling Voss
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: handsome middle-aged man, broad shoulders, silver-streaked dark hair styled with precision, clean-shaven square jaw, idealized superhero physique, form-fitting blue-and-gold superhero suit with cape, confident cocky expression
dialogue_examples: I don't need a PR rescue. I need a city worthy of my brand.
Relax. I've survived bullets, blades, and three ex-wives. This town is just another abusive relationship.
If the mayor wants a headline, he'll get one. I do my best work in front of cameras.
I was born to save this city; unfortunately, it was not born with taste.

character_data.name: Velma "Volt" Reyes
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: fit Black woman in her late 20s, short undercut dyed copper, athletic build, bright sharp eyes, sleek red-and-silver speedster suit with practical boots and fingerless gloves, restless smirk
dialogue_examples: Captain, if you flex any harder, the windows are going to file for harassment.
I can outrun a riot, but apparently not this meeting.
If the mayor's lying, he should at least make it sexy.
I hit like a truck and negotiate like a knife fight. Pick your favorite.

character_data.name: Dr. Hazel Hex
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: pale woman in her early 30s, slender build, long black hair in a loose braid, sharp cheekbones, dark lipstick, subtle arcane tattoos, high-collared tactical coat over occult-tech attire, cool deadpan expression
dialogue_examples: Your confidence is deeply expensive and almost certainly uninsured.
I bind cursed circuitry, not egos, though tonight I may need to improvise.
The city isn't broken. It's been profit-tested.
If we survive this, I'm invoicing everyone for emotional damages.

character_data.name: Brick Dain
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: very large muscular man in his 30s, tan skin, close-cropped hair, broad jaw, warm brown eyes, heavy armored superhero suit with reinforced plates and sleeveless design, calm friendly expression
dialogue_examples: I don't do subtle. I do impact.
If the plan needs a battering ram, I'm emotionally available.
I may not understand the politics, but I know a rat when I smell one.
I used to be a bouncer. This team is somehow worse.

character_data.name: Mayor Basil Venn
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: well-groomed middle-aged man, thin and polished build, silver hair perfectly styled, sharp nose, expensive tailored suit, polished cufflinks, smug politician smile, oily charisma
dialogue_examples: I'm simply giving the public what it deserves: reassurance and a tasteful amount of fear.
Heroes are lovely, but crises are better for turnout.
You're not corrupt if you call it civic optimization.
I adore a scandal. It keeps the electorate hydrated.

location_data.name: Sentinel Syndicate HQ
location_data.location_image_prompt: sleek corporate superhero headquarters interior with polished white floors, glass walls, chrome accents, a large tactical city map display, trophy cases, training mats, stacked equipment crates, scattered paperwork, and a half-broken vending machine

location_data.name: Neon Wharf District
location_data.location_image_prompt: busy waterfront district at night with neon signs, wet pavement, graffiti-covered brick buildings, dock cranes, chain-link fences, food carts, overhead rail tracks, cable bundles, and dense urban clutter

location_data.name: Gilded Spire City Hall
location_data.location_image_prompt: ornate municipal interior with marble columns, brass railings, polished floors, civic murals, podiums, flags, security checkpoints, and a grand press area

location_data.name: Blackwater Substation
location_data.location_image_prompt: abandoned underground substation with rusted transformers, cracked concrete, exposed pipes, tangled cables, grated walkways, warning placards, and hidden high-tech equipment bolted into the industrial ruin

scene_data.narrtive_summary: Riley Park arrives for their first day as the Sentinel Syndicate's new crisis manager and immediately walks into a post-battle disaster: the team has just embarrassed itself on live media, the HQ is a mess, and everyone has a different idea about who is to blame. Captain Voss tries to charm his way through the fallout, Volt needles the newcomers, Hazel studies the situation like a crime scene, and Brick offers sincere but unhelpful encouragement. The scene establishes Port Meridian's media-obsessed superhero culture and the threat of Mayor Basil Venn cutting the team's funding if their public image does not improve.

Riley mediates the team's first formal briefing, learning that the Syndicate is less a unit than a soap opera with branding. An anonymous tip about illegal municipal transfers and staged supervillain incidents lands in the room, and the heroes argue over whether to investigate or just pose better. Riley steers them toward a low-level street response in Neon Wharf to improve public standing while secretly following the money trail.

What looks like a routine mugging in the Neon Wharf District turns out to be a deliberately staged distraction with hired muscle, tampered comms, and enough panic to make the news cycle salivate. Riley helps the team keep civilians safe while the heroes bicker in the middle of the fight. The battle ends with a captured henchman and evidence that the scam is tied to City Hall through a private contractor account.

At a glittering City Hall fundraiser, Riley navigates politics, vanity, and predatory small talk while the team tries to pass as competent adults in public. Mayor Basil Venn performs civic charm like a stage magician and quietly needles the Syndicate with hints that their charter, funding, and reputations are all one unfavorable vote away from collapse. Riley and Hazel spot signs that the mayor is funneling money into a privatized emergency response scheme, and the investigation points toward an off-book facility beneath the city.

The team infiltrates the Blackwater Substation and discovers a hidden broadcast rig, emotion-amplifying tech, and hard proof that many recent crises were manufactured for political theater. The heroes' frustration boils over into a near-breakup as Voss admits he has been playing the public-image game for years, while Volt and Hazel accuse him of making everyone complicit. Riley forces a temporary truce, secures the evidence, and triggers the mayor's security response, turning the whole city into a locked-down pressure cooker.

Back at HQ, the team finally stops shouting long enough to plan. Riley turns their mess into a coordinated heist, assigning each hero a role in a live-broadcast counterattack against the mayor's coming address. The scene focuses on the uneasy but genuine emotional shift from dysfunctional rivals to a squad that can, at last, trust one another to follow through on a bad idea.

During Mayor Venn's live citywide address, the Syndicate hijacks the broadcast and exposes the manufactured-crisis scheme in front of the entire city. Riley and the heroes turn the mayor's own spectacle back on him, using timing, chaos, and just enough restraint to keep the room from exploding socially or literally. Venn's polished persona cracks, the public finally sees the scam, and the team pulls off a rare, synchronized victory.

With Venn exposed and Port Meridian briefly less awful, the city settles into its usual noisy, hungover state. The team celebrates a messy victory, acknowledges that they are still a disaster, and quietly admits they function better when Riley is around to keep the wheels on. Riley is offered a permanent place with the Sentinel Syndicate, leaving the story on a note of reluctant belonging, with the promise that tomorrow will probably be worse.

beat_sheets.source_scene_summary: Riley Park arrives for their first day as the Sentinel Syndicate's new crisis manager and immediately walks into a post-battle disaster: the team has just embarrassed itself on live media, the HQ is a mess, and everyone has a different idea about who is to blame. Captain Voss tries to charm his way through the fallout, Volt needles the newcomers, Hazel studies the situation like a crime scene, and Brick offers sincere but unhelpful encouragement. The scene establishes Port Meridian's media-obsessed superhero culture and the threat of Mayor Basil Venn cutting the team's funding if their public image does not improve.
beat_sheets.dramatic_question: Can Riley impose any order on the Sentinel Syndicate before their latest public humiliation costs them funding, authority, and whatever dignity they still have left?
beat_sheets.scene_goal: Survive the first day by understanding what went wrong, stopping the team's blame spiral, and establishing enough authority to keep the Syndicate from collapsing under bad optics.
beat_sheets.scene_turn: Riley arrives expecting a management job and leaves the scene as the de facto adult in a politically endangered circus, with the team's future suddenly resting on their ability to control the chaos.

beats.purpose: Establish the setting, tone, and immediate scale of the disaster Riley has inherited.
beats.summary: Riley enters Sentinel Syndicate HQ to find a polished superhero brand space wrecked by a recent response and buzzing with screens replaying the team's public embarrassment. News chatter and visible damage make it instantly clear that this city treats heroics as both emergency service and entertainment product.
beats.revelation: The Syndicate has just suffered a very public on-air failure, and HQ is still physically and reputationally smoking from it.
beats.player_goal: Get oriented and assess what kind of disaster this new job actually is.
beats.exit_state: Riley understands before speaking to anyone that their first day has begun in the middle of a PR catastrophe.

beats.purpose: Introduce the core team dynamics and showcase each hero's default coping mechanism.
beats.summary: Captain Voss swoops in with polished charm and confident spin, trying to frame the fiasco as a minor optics issue rather than a structural collapse. Volt immediately pokes at Riley, Hazel silently inspects damage and footage like evidence, and Brick offers earnest support that is heartfelt but not especially clarifying.
beats.revelation: The Syndicate is not functioning like a unified team; it is functioning like several incompatible personal brands sharing a lease.
beats.player_goal: Figure out who these people are and whether any of them can be managed.
beats.exit_state: Riley gets a rapid, alarming read on the personalities they are expected to organize.

beats.purpose: Demonstrate the team's dysfunction in action and make the operational problem concrete.
beats.summary: Riley attempts a simple post-incident debrief, but it immediately mutates into overlapping accusations about grandstanding, bad calls, missing support, and collateral damage. Hazel's cool observations and Brick's honest admissions only make the picture worse: nobody agrees on what happened, and nobody trusts anyone else's version.
beats.revelation: The field failure involved botched coordination, media-friendly collateral, and possibly missing comms support, but the team is too fractured to produce a reliable account.
beats.player_goal: Extract a usable timeline before the argument becomes a civil war.
beats.exit_state: The debrief collapses, proving that the Syndicate's real crisis is internal.

beats.purpose: Give the player a first leadership choice and establish Riley's ability to redirect the scene.
beats.summary: Riley cuts through the shouting and forces the room to focus, whether by sharp authority, deadpan humor, or practical triage language. The team does not suddenly become cooperative, but they do pause long enough to acknowledge that the new crisis manager may be harder to ignore than expected.
beats.revelation: The team responds to confidence and control more readily than to rank or procedure.
beats.player_goal: Stop the blame spiral and establish personal credibility.
beats.choice_prompt: How does Riley shut down the argument and claim authority?
branch_outcomes: Riley goes formal and strict, earning wary respect while provoking Voss's ego.
Riley uses dry humor to puncture the drama, winning Volt's interest but lowering the room's sense of ceremony.
Riley appeals to shared stakes and practical survival, calming Brick and Hazel while pushing the team toward reluctant cooperation.
beats.exit_state: Riley takes control of the conversation, if not yet the team.

beats.purpose: Seed the larger conspiracy tone without overtaking the immediate scene conflict.
beats.summary: With the room briefly calmer, Hazel notes details that don't sit right: camera crews arrived absurdly fast, comms died at a convenient moment, and the footage seems almost too perfectly humiliating. Volt treats this as typical Port Meridian opportunism, but Riley starts to see that the city runs on a grotesque synergy between danger, publicity, and profit.
beats.revelation: The team's humiliation may have been amplified by suspiciously convenient media timing, hinting that Port Meridian's crisis culture is more engineered than it looks.
beats.player_goal: Determine whether today's fiasco was just incompetence or something more calculated.
beats.exit_state: Riley's job expands from cleanup to quiet suspicion.

beats.purpose: Introduce the external political threat and frame the stakes for the next act.
beats.summary: A polished message from Mayor Basil Venn hits HQ, all civic concern and velvet-edged menace, warning that the Syndicate's funding and charter are under review after today's embarrassment. Voss's charm curdles, Volt bristles, Brick visibly deflates, and Hazel absorbs the threat with icy focus.
beats.revelation: If the Syndicate's public image does not improve immediately, City Hall can reduce or revoke the support keeping them operational.
beats.player_goal: Understand the real consequences of this disaster and what must be stabilized first.
beats.exit_state: The team's infighting is suddenly overshadowed by the possibility of institutional collapse.

beats.purpose: End the scene with Riley's commitment and a new working status quo.
beats.summary: Riley bluntly reframes the situation: this is no longer about hurt feelings or heroic vanity, but about surviving the next news cycle with a budget and a mandate intact. Nobody looks happy about it, but nobody has a better plan either, and Riley's first day ends with the team grudgingly accepting that they need a handler more than a cheerleader.
beats.revelation: The team is dysfunctional enough, and endangered enough, to tolerate Riley taking a real coordinating role.
beats.player_goal: Set the terms for working together and survive day one with authority intact.
beats.exit_state: Riley becomes the de facto stabilizing force at HQ, and the story is poised to move into active crisis management.

beat_sheets.source_scene_summary: Riley mediates the team's first formal briefing, learning that the Syndicate is less a unit than a soap opera with branding. An anonymous tip about illegal municipal transfers and staged supervillain incidents lands in the room, and the heroes argue over whether to investigate or just pose better. Riley steers them toward a low-level street response in Neon Wharf to improve public standing while secretly following the money trail.
beat_sheets.dramatic_question: Can Riley turn the Syndicate's first real briefing from a vanity riot into a workable mission before a potentially explosive corruption lead slips away?
beat_sheets.scene_goal: Establish authority over the team, stop the briefing from imploding, and choose a response that protects public optics while quietly testing the anonymous tip.
beat_sheets.scene_turn: What begins as post-disaster damage control becomes the Syndicate's first covert step toward uncovering a City Hall conspiracy.

beats.purpose: Establish Riley's first formal attempt to manage the team and immediately showcase how dysfunctional the Syndicate is.
beats.summary: Riley opens the briefing in HQ expecting a basic after-action meeting and instead gets a five-way collision of ego, sarcasm, and avoidance. Voss performs leadership for an imaginary camera, Volt heckles, Hazel silently judges the room like a forensic examiner, and Brick tries to be helpful without actually imposing order.
beats.revelation: The Syndicate has no real internal discipline; it functions on personality inertia and public-facing bluff.
beats.player_goal: Get the room under control long enough to begin a real briefing.
beats.exit_state: The meeting is technically underway, but Riley now understands that basic structure will have to be enforced, not assumed.

beats.purpose: Let the player define Riley's early management style while surfacing each hero's competing priorities.
beats.summary: Riley asks for a recap of the last fiasco, and the team instantly turns it into a blame contest. The player chooses how Riley cuts through the noise, shaping whether the room responds best to authority, competence, deflection, or blunt practicality.
beats.revelation: Each hero defines success differently: Voss cares about optics, Volt about action, Hazel about facts, and Brick about keeping people safe.
beats.player_goal: Contain the blame spiral and extract something useful from it.
beats.choice_prompt: How does Riley seize control of the briefing?
branch_outcomes: Challenge Voss's grandstanding first, establishing backbone but bruising his ego.
Back Hazel's fact-based approach, earning her respect while making the meeting colder and sharper.
Redirect Volt's mockery into actionable input, winning some momentum but inviting more attitude.
Ask Brick for the plain version, grounding the room in practical details and lowering the temperature.
beats.exit_state: Riley earns just enough authority to keep the briefing from dissolving into pure theater.

beats.purpose: Introduce the inciting clue that transforms the scene from team comedy into conspiracy setup.
beats.summary: An anonymous encrypted tip drops into the Syndicate's intake feed mid-briefing, carrying municipal transfer records, shell-account fragments, and a claim that some recent villain incidents were staged. Hazel verifies that the scrubbed metadata is sophisticated, which makes the message feel less like fan mail and more like a live wire.
beats.revelation: City money may be flowing through a private contractor tied to manufactured emergencies.
beats.player_goal: Figure out whether the anonymous tip is a prank, a trap, or a real lead.
beats.exit_state: The room's focus shifts from embarrassment over bad press to the possibility of systemic corruption.

beats.purpose: Force the team's core ideological split into the open: truth versus optics.
beats.summary: The tip ignites a fresh argument about what the Syndicate is even for. Voss warns that chasing anonymous accusations will make them look desperate, Volt wants to run straight at the scandal, Brick admits the whole thing smells wrong, and Hazel points out that fake crises would explain too many convenient patterns.
beats.revelation: The team has no shared doctrine for choosing between public image and actual investigation.
beats.player_goal: Keep the team from splitting into camps before any decision is made.
beats.exit_state: Riley realizes the real job is not just reading the lead, but packaging a direction the team will actually follow.

beats.purpose: Give Riley a practical, deniable way to pursue the tip and begin a quiet alignment with Hazel.
beats.summary: While the argument keeps Voss and Volt occupied, Riley and Hazel cross-check the transfer data against current dispatch chatter. A low-level disturbance in Neon Wharf overlaps with one of the contractor names in the tip, turning a routine response into a possible field test of the money trail.
beats.revelation: Neon Wharf may be where the suspicious municipal transfers connect to street-level incidents.
beats.player_goal: Find a lead the team can pursue without announcing a conspiracy hunt to the whole room.
beats.exit_state: Riley now has a mission target that can function as both public service and covert investigation.

beats.purpose: Create the scene's main tactical choice by letting Riley decide how to sell the Neon Wharf mission.
beats.summary: Riley proposes sending the team to Neon Wharf, but the pitch can be framed in several ways depending on what will move this specific circus. The player's choice determines whether the mission is sold primarily as optics repair, routine civic duty, or a plausible investigative lead, shifting who feels most validated by Riley's call.
beats.revelation: The same mission can satisfy radically different motives if Riley frames it correctly.
beats.player_goal: Get unanimous enough buy-in to move the team out the door.
beats.choice_prompt: How does Riley pitch the Neon Wharf response?
branch_outcomes: Frame it as a clean PR win, making Voss cooperative while Volt openly mocks the spin.
Frame it as a suspicious lead worth testing, energizing Volt and Hazel while making Voss wary.
Frame it as simple public duty, earning Brick's approval and reducing open resistance without revealing Riley's suspicion.
beats.exit_state: The team commits to the Neon Wharf call, even if each member thinks they're going for a different reason.

beats.purpose: End the scene with forward motion, clear team roles, and Riley's private investigative intent.
beats.summary: With the argument finally channeled into action, Riley assigns roles: Voss handles the visible heroics, Volt covers rapid response, Brick protects civilians, and Hazel monitors tech anomalies. As they mobilize, Riley quietly keeps a copy of the transfer data, deciding to follow the money under cover of the team's sudden burst of civic responsibility.
beats.revelation: Riley can use the Syndicate's obsession with optics as cover for a real investigation.
beats.player_goal: Launch the mission without tipping the team into another argument or exposing the full suspicion too early.
beats.exit_state: The Syndicate heads to Neon Wharf for what looks like a low-stakes public assist, while Riley privately begins tracking a conspiracy.
